preFix.title: Roman script families
preFix.subtitle: A survey of the main Western script hands, from monumental capitals to humanist minuscule, and how they relate to each other
preFix.excerpt: Western calligraphy comprises a handful of major script families, each with a distinct historical moment and a distinct visual character. Roman capitals, uncial, Carolingian, gothic blackletter, humanist italic, and copperplate are the main branches. Understanding where each came from and how one grew out of another gives the calligrapher a map for choosing the right hand for any given commission.
preFix.sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing & Illuminating & Lettering (1906), chapter on historical survey of scripts. Internet Archive: https://archive.org/details/writingilluminat00john. Also drawn from: Alfred Fairbank, A Handwriting Manual (1932), public domain.

content.text: Western calligraphy runs in a continuous line from Roman inscription capitals of the 1st century AD to the copperplate hands of the 18th century. Each major script grew out of what came before, simplified, elaborated, or reformed. The six families below are the chapters of that story.
Roman capitals
The monumental
majuscule

script, cut in stone with a broad chisel, is the root of the Western alphabet. The proportions of Trajan's Column (c. 113 AD) are the source of every roman typeface and every foundational hand. Calligraphy derived from the inscriptions uses a broad-edge pen at a shallow angle (about 30°) to approximate the chiselled thick-thin contrast. Roman capitals are formal, slow, and suited to display headlines, dedication panels, and monumental lettering.
Uncial

Uncial script (4th-9th centuries) is a rounded book hand with a nearly flat nib angle, giving very shallow thick-thin contrast. Letters are large and open, and look like lower-case even at capital size: the forms a, d, e, g, h, m, n, q, and r are uncial inventions. These scripts are entirely
minuscule
in character. Half-uncial pushed the ascenders and descenders further, giving the first true lower-case.
Carolingian minuscule

Charlemagne's court scribes produced a clear, upright lower-case hand in the late 8th century: Carolingian minuscule. It was the first hand to pair true lower-case letters with Roman capitals in one alphabet. Open counters, even spacing, and consistent heights make it highly legible. Italian humanists later rediscovered it and called it the
humanist script
Gothic blackletter

From the 12th century, the pressure to copy more books faster pushed scribes to compress and angularise Carolingian forms. The result was blackletter: dense, closely spaced text with strong verticals and minimal curves. Gothic textura quadrata is the most formal sub-family; cursiva and bastarda are quicker variants. Gothic is used today for display headlines, certificates, and anything needing a historical or Germanic feel.
Italic

Italian humanist scribes in the 15th century revived Carolingian minuscule as a classical alternative to gothic. A slightly sloped, faster version called chancery cursive became the working hand of Renaissance courts. It was named italic after its Italian origin. Italic is the most versatile broad-edge hand: readable at small sizes and suited to formal and informal use alike. It is where most calligraphers start.
Copperplate

The development of intaglio engraving in the 16th century allowed scribes to translate their pen work into copper-engraved copy-books. The engraver's burin produced hairlines and swelling shades that no broad-edge nib could match, and a new class of pointed-flex nib was developed to replicate the effect in ink. Copperplate (also called English roundhand or Spencerian) is a pressure-and-release script: hairlines on the upstroke, shaded ovals on the down. It is the ancestor of modern wedding calligraphy.
Choosing a script for a commission
Monumental, historical, or institutional text: Roman capitals or Carolingian.
Medieval, religious, or Germanic register: Gothic blackletter.
General readable calligraphy, dedication panels, quotations: Italic.
Wedding stationery, envelope addressing, invitation suites: Copperplate or Spencerian.
